final_payload.description: To satisfy the preconditions for StubHttpRequest.cookieValue(name), the argument must be a non-null cookie name that exists in the parsed cookie list returned by cookies(), which itself is derived from this.headers containing at least one "Cookie" header entry. Setting this.headers to include a first "Cookie" header whose value is a crafted cookie header string with duplicate cookie names and conflicting attributes (notably multiple Max-Age values) can trigger the known CookieDecoder parsing inconsistency where the attribute parsing overflows and causes getMaxAge() to return Integer.MIN_VALUE. With arg0 set to the duplicated cookie name, cookieValue(arg0) will call cookie(arg0), iterate cookies(), find the first matching cookie by name, and return its value, completing the execution path under the required conditions while carrying the malicious header content that triggers the decoder issue upstream when cookies() is computed.

final_payload.description: HttpRequestWrapper.cookieValue(name) is a pure delegate to this.request.cookieValue(name). The downstream implementation (shown in Helper Methods) resolves a cookie by name from the list produced by parsing the inbound Cookie header(s) and returns cookie.getValue(). To satisfy the layer goal and align with the sink-level constraints, we set this.request to a non-null org.webbitserver.stub.StubHttpRequest whose headers list contains a Cookie header value crafted with duplicate cookies of the same name and conflicting attribute-like tokens (e.g., $Path and Max-Age). This style of payload is known to confuse some Cookie decoders/parsers that incorrectly treat attribute tokens as separate cookies or merge them inconsistently across duplicates, which can lead to an integer overflow when computing/reading Max-Age such that getMaxAge() returns Integer.MIN_VALUE. The provided name parameter must be non-null and match at least one parsed HttpCookie name; using the duplicated cookie name ensures InboundCookieParser.parse returns a list containing at least one HttpCookie with getName()==name, allowing cookieValue(name) to traverse the vulnerable parsing path and return a value for that cookie.

final_payload.description: To satisfy the sink-level constraint at this layer, the HttpUriRequest must return a java.net.URI whose *user-info* contains one or more '@' characters, creating an ambiguous authority section. A concrete example is an HTTP URI where the credentials look like "user@attacker:pass@victim:80". When downstream code (outside this method) uses Apache HttpClient URI parsing utilities (e.g., URIUtils.extractHost()) on such a URI, the extra '@' in user-info can cause the authority to be misinterpreted, potentially yielding a null/incorrect HttpHost. In this method, the request is executed and on non-200 status it logs request.getURI(); that ensures the crafted URI is actually materialized and propagated. The context can be null (no effect here), and charset must be a valid encoding (UTF-8). Additionally, httpClient and logger must be non-null for normal execution.

final_payload.description: The method run() takes no parameters and only calls execute() inside a try/catch. To satisfy the layer goal, we must control the instance field 'folder' because the only concrete dataflow shown here is in the IOException handler: it prints folder.get(0). The global sink-level constraint requires that folder[0] be the exact malicious string that triggers FilenameUtils.normalize() to return null when given a Windows UNC path prefix combined with traversal segments (..\\). A concrete UNC+traversal payload like "\\\\attacker\\share\\..\\..\\windows\\system32" matches that pattern (UNC prefix "\\\\attacker\\share\\" followed by "..\\..\\" traversal). If execute() later passes this string into FilenameUtils.normalize(), the normalization edge-case can yield null; if that null is not handled properly downstream, it can lead to path traversal or incorrect path handling. This layer’s precondition is met by setting 'folder' to a single-element List containing exactly that payload.

final_payload.description: At this layer, HttpRequestWrapper.cookies() simply delegates to this.request.cookies(). The underlying StubHttpRequest.cookies() implementation (per helper source) calls InboundCookieParser.parse(headers("Cookie")). Therefore the only requirement to drive the vulnerable parsing behavior is that this.request is a StubHttpRequest whose headers list contains at least one entry with key exactly "Cookie" and a crafted cookie header string value. The provided concrete Cookie header includes multiple cookie definitions with the same name ("SID") and conflicting attribute-style assignments (including duplicate $Path and a Max-Age that overflows 32-bit int). This combination is intended to confuse attribute association in the decoder (treating some attribute tokens as belonging to the wrong cookie instance), leading to an integer overflow when Max-Age is parsed/converted such that getMaxAge() can return Integer.MIN_VALUE.

final_payload.description: In cookieValue(name), the input arg0 is only used to select which cookie to return from cookie(name). The helper cookie(name) iterates over cookies() and returns the first HttpCookie whose getName().equals(arg0). To ensure the downstream vulnerable CookieDecoder behavior described in the Sink-Level Requirement, the request must contain a Cookie header string that defines multiple cookies with the same name but with conflicting attribute assignments (notably different $Path values and a Max-Age that overflows). When cookies() parses this header, it will produce at least one HttpCookie with name equal to arg0, so cookie(name) returns it and cookieValue returns its value, propagating the malformed/ambiguously-parsed cookie instance into later logic that calls getMaxAge() and triggers the integer overflow (returning Integer.MIN_VALUE) due to the decoder mis-parsing attributes across duplicated cookie definitions.

final_payload.description: HttpRequestWrapper.cookieValue(name) is a thin delegator that forwards the provided cookie name to the underlying NettyHttpRequest.cookieValue(name). To reach the vulnerable parsing behavior described in the sink-level constraints, the underlying DefaultHttpRequest must carry a 'Cookie' header whose first header value is a single cookie header string containing multiple definitions of the same cookie name with conflicting attribute-style tokens (e.g., duplicated $Path assignments). Passing arg0 as that duplicated cookie name causes Netty's cookie decoder logic (invoked by request.cookieValue(name)) to parse the Cookie header and encounter inconsistent attribute parsing for the duplicated cookie, which in the vulnerable version leads to a malformed max-age computation and an integer overflow such that getMaxAge() returns Integer.MIN_VALUE. This layer’s contribution is selecting the cookie name that forces the decoder to process the problematic duplicate-cookie entries.
